coredata.dc:title: First-principles studies the optical properties of defective-GQDs and -fullerene
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Yongqi
zhang, Qi
Bai, Lingling
Ye, Lingfeng
Lin, Zhiping
Dong, Huafeng
Wu, Fugen
coredata.dc:description: We demonstrate the size effect of quantum dots and discuss the effect of defect on the luminescence properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and fullerene (C60) in this paper. Results show that the smaller the GQDs, the more difficult it is to prepared. The absorption peak of visible light region can be attained from large-size GQDs and small-size GQDs mainly show the luminescence performance of UV region. The band gap of GQDs become narrower and narrower with increasing size and adjusting symmetry. And then C60 (0.7 nm) with higher symmetry exhibits better optical and electronic conductivity. The defective GQDs and C60 have better adsorption capacity for functional group –OH and -NMP than non-defective structure. And the effect of defect and adsorbed functional group on the optical properties is clearly different, which C defect and functional group adsorption can improve its optical properties for large size GQDs and completely weaken its optical properties for C60.
coredata.dcterms:subject: GQDs
C60 quantum dots
Optical properties
Electronic properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The optical properties of non-defective GQDs and C60 cage
The optical properties of GQDs and C60 adsorbing functional groups –OH and -NMP

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical absorption spectrum of different sizes of GQDs, which size is from 0.5 nm to 1.6 nm. Here, 1.1 nm-a GQDs are correspond to 6 rings with low symmetry and 1.1 nm-b to 7 rings with high symmetry, respectively.
Total density of states of different sizes of GQDs and the width of band gap.
The optical properties and electronic density of states of C60 quantum dots. (a) is the optical adsorption spectrum; (b) is the band gap of C60.
Charge density differences for the (a) defect-graphite sheet + OH, (b) defect-graphite sheet + NMP, (c) defect-C60+OH, (d) defect-C60+NMP.
Optical absorption spectra of GQDs and C60 adsorbing OH and NMP functional group. (a) GQDs and (b) C60.
The formation enthalpy of different sizes of GQDs and C60.
Adsorption energy of OH/NMP on defective sites for GQDs and C60, as well as nondefective structure.

head.ce:title: First-principles studies the optical properties of defective-GQDs and -fullerene

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Guangdong University of Technology, Guangzhou, 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Guangdong University of Technology, Guangzhou 510006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials and Energies, Guangdong University of Technology, Guangzhou, 510006, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials and Energies, Guangdong University of Technology, Guangzhou 510006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Province Key Laboratory of Intelligent Decision and Cooperative Control, Guangzhou, 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Province Key Laboratory of Intelligent Decision and Cooperative Control

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Province Key Laboratory of Intelligent Decision and Cooperative Control, Guangzhou 510006, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We demonstrate the size effect of quantum dots and discuss the effect of defect on the luminescence properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and fullerene (C60) in this paper. Results show that the smaller the GQDs, the more difficult it is to prepared. The absorption peak of visible light region can be attained from large-size GQDs and small-size GQDs mainly show the luminescence performance of UV region. The band gap of GQDs become narrower and narrower with increasing size and adjusting symmetry. And then C60 (0.7 nm) with higher symmetry exhibits better optical and electronic conductivity. The defective GQDs and C60 have better adsorption capacity for functional group –OH and -NMP than non-defective structure. And the effect of defect and adsorbed functional group on the optical properties is clearly different, which C defect and functional group adsorption can improve its optical properties for large size GQDs and completely weaken its optical properties for C60.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nonadots and their derivatives have great potential in biomedical applications, owing to their superior solubility, chemical stability, and low toxicity, as well as their exceptional optical properties of multicolor emissions, wide absorptivity, and upconverting light emission [1,2]. In terms of quantum yield, carbon dots' photoluminescence capabilities are still inferior to commercial silicon dots [3]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are nanometer-sized fragments of graphene, which have been used in bandgap applications owing to strong quantum confinement and edge effects producing exceptional fluorescence properties [4–6]. For different tailorable control, GQDs show interesting optical properties. The absorption bands of GQDs is located the ultraviolet region for sizes ranging from a few to dozens nanomaters. And then regulating its size and surface chemical states [7,8] can change its electronic properties and fluorescence properties. One-step chemical oxidation method for rupturing C60 molecules in GOLQDs have highly soluble in water and strong photoluminescence [9], which is related to the size and emissive traps caused by oxygen-containing groups. Some surface functional groups intrinsically formed during the fabrication process, such as epoxy (C–O–C), hydroxyl (-OH), and carboxyl groups (-COOH) on the basal plane and at the edges of carbon quantum dots, can often affect its characteristics [1,10]. L. Wang et al. [11] have been investigated carbon quantum dots adsorbing carboxyl and hydroxyl groups and found the common association between green luminescence and certain surface functional groups. Recent research has focused on determining and controlling the influence of various oxygen related groups on GQDs and their derivatives. The functionalized GQDs with amine group exhibit a red-shift of photoluminescence emission that can be changed by adjusting the pH value [12]. So far, it has been reported that adjustment of oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing functional groups can control the emission color [13–15]. It is crucial to understand the effect of the optical properties of oxygen-containing functional groups adsorbed on GQDs.

The surface energy traps further emphasize the impact of the surface condition on the energy gap of carbon quantum dots. Bao et al. [16]. have been reported that surface defects induced by surface oxidation can serve as capture centers for exciton and cause a narrow band gap, by which the band gap between the LUMO and HOMO can be reduced with increasing oxidation amounts on surfaces. Tetsuka et al. [17] have been integrated various nitrogen-containing functional groups that can alter the HOMO/LUMO energy levels. Topological stone-wales type GQDs with divacancy can harvest both singlet and triplet excitions, and the position of divacancy determines the tunability range of the emission wavelengths [18]. Here, we will investigate the optical properties of different size GQDs, which can evaluate the quantum size effect on the optical properties. In addition, we also design GQDs and C60 with carbon vacancy defects, as well as that of adsorbed hydroxyl groups (OH) and 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidinone (NMP) on their defect site. Results can show how the change of optical properties along with adsorbing functional groups.

Calculations are performed by VASP code [19,20]. All calculations were performed by Perdew–Burke– Ernzerhof (PBE) parameterization of the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) for exchange-correlation potential [21]. The 1s1 in hydrogen (H), 2s2 2p2 in carbon (C), 2s2 2p3 in nitrogen (N), 2s2 2p4 in oxygen (O) atoms are treated as the valence states. Two types of defect structure are studied, namely defect-GQDs and defect-C60. From the perspective of theoretical calculation, tailorabled graphene sheets used in our research range from the smallest 1 ring to 19 rings with 72 atoms. And the boundaries of all graphene sheets were modified with hydrogen atoms to simulate laboratory-prepared GQDs. The calculated cell in which GQDs and C60 is located in 30 × 30 × 50 Å room, which is large enough to avoid interactions between the neighboring cells. The system automatically assigns Monkhorst-Pack k-points to map the Brillouin zone [22]. Bader charge was applied to discuss the charge transfer between different atoms [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optical properties of non-defective GQDs and C60 cage
ce:section.ce:para:
The formation of energy is an important parameter to describe the stability of the material. It is well known, material systems with lower formation energy are more stable than that with higher formation energy. The formation enthalpy of binary compound is given by following equation [24].
Δ H
(
A p
B q
)
= E
(
A p
B q
)
−
[
p
E
e q
( A )
+ q
E
e q
( B )
]

E
(
A p
B q
)
is the total energy of the
A p
B q
compound.
E
e q
( A )
and
E
e q
( B )
are the per atom energies of the compound under equilibrium state. p and q is the composition of the compound, respectively.

Table .1 shows calculated formation enthalpies for different sizes GQDs and C60. For different size GQDs, results show an consistent tendency. The formation energies are all strongly negative for all GQDs, which is favoring for its formation. And when the larger sizes with 19 ring (1.6 nm), there is lower formation energy (−7.236eV). The boundary of graphene sheet is modified with hydrogen atoms and three-dimensional spatial vacuum layer. And then discussed graphene sheets are very close to laboratory-prepared GQDs, which are usually dissolved in organic solvents [25]. The larger the size, the lower the formation energy. And then the formation of different size GQDs become more and more stable with the scale increasing. That is, the smaller the GQDs, the more difficult it is to prepared. So, it is reasonable for preparing different size GQDs. C60 is also called fullerene, which is a football-like 32-hedron that includes 20 hexagons and 12 pentagons. C60 is the most stable arrangement of the chemical bond. And its size is approximately 0.7 nm, which is with consistent dimension of GQDs with two ring. Its formation energy (14.12eV) is large, and the preparation is very difficult as quantum dot structure.

The interaction of an incident photon with an electron can be described according to the time dependent perturbations of the electronic ground state. And the complex dielectric function of material can reflect the information of optical transition. The complex dielectric function is given by the following equation
ε
( ω )
=
ε 1
+ i
ε 2
. The magnitude of ε 2 is calculated from the momentum matrix elements between the valence and conduction band wave function [26]. The real part ε 1 of the dielectric function can be calculated by the Kramers–Kronig relationship [27]. Meanwhile, the other optical parameters such as the reflectivity
R
( ω )
and absorption
α
( ω )
can be determined using the values of ε 1 and ε 2 [28]. And the absorption coefficient
α
( ω )
can be obtained from ε 1(ω) and ε 2(ω), which is expressed following as
α
( ω )
=
2
ω
[
ε 1 2
( ω )
+
ε 2 2
( ω )
−
ε 1
( ω )
]
1 2
.Fig. 1 shows the optical properties of different sizes of GQDs. The size of GQDs is from 0.5 nm to 1.6 nm. The smallest GQDs (0.5 nm) with 1 ring do not present any optical properties. The absorption spectrum of 0.7 nm GQDs have a distinct absorption peak in wavelength 295 nm, which is located in ultraviolet (UV). And there are two satellite peaks in primary peak, 256 nm and 366 nm respectively. For 0.9 nm GQDs, it can be seen that the intensity of absorption peak 295 nm decrease and another two satellite peaks increase. Along with size increasing 1.1 nm, the absorption peak 295 nm has completely disappeared and the satellite peak on both sides have become the main peak. The peak intensity located in deep ultraviolet (DVU) (409 nm for 1.1 nm-a and 425 nm for 1.1 nm-b) became stronger and stronger and appears blue shift. From the perspective of the symmetry, 1.1 nm-b with higher symmetry shows greater intensity and narrower peak width than 1.1 nm-a with low symmetry. When increased 1.6 nm, the intensity of all peaks have a relatively significant reduction. So, high-symmetry GQDs has a better optical performance. And there is a new peak located in red light area, which maxi-peak value is 650 nm. Hence, the absorption peak of large-size GQDs can be attained in visible range of light which may be interesting for visible-light applications. In contrast, small-size GQDs mainly show the luminescence performance of UV and DUV region.

Fig. 2 show the TDOS of different sizes of GQDs, which can feedback the electronic conductivity rooting in the size of GQDs. It can be seen that the band gap of GQDs with increasing size become narrower and narrower. The band gap of smallest GQDs (0.5 nm) is 5.26eV. And increasing size from 0.5 nm to 1.6 nm, the band gap of GQDs will decrease from 5.26eV to 1.02eV. The VBM is located in −0.5eV for all different size GQDs and do not shift along with GQDs size change. And the change of band is originate from the shift of CBM gap, which shift from 4.76eV to 0.52eV. There are two different symmetric 1.1 nm GQDs, which show different band gap width. Low symmetric 1.1 nm-a GQDs have narrower band gap (1.52eV), and higher symmetric 1.1 nm-b GQDs have wider band gap (1.92eV). Therefore, for same size GQDs the symmetry will affect the band gap. The higher the symmetry, the wider the band gap. Fig.S1 shows PDOS of different size GQDs. It can be seen that electronic structure come from the hybirdization of s, px, py and pz orbital electrons, and change of band gap for different size GQDs is mainly caused by pz orbital electron. C60 have higher symmetry and smaller size (0.7 nm, which is about 2 rings GQDs). And then C60 is much closer to the idealized quantum dots. Its electronic properties and optical obsorption spectrum have been calculated and shown in Fig. 3 . Its band gap is 0.705eV and much smaller than the same size GQDs. Furthermore, it is smaller than larger 1.6 nm GQDs. And then increasing structural symmetry of quantum dots can effectively reduce band gap width and improve conductivity. For C60, higher symmetry not only affect its electronic properties but also optical properties. Fig. 3(a) show that there are three adsorbing peaks located on different region, which is deep ultraviolet (DUV), ultraviolet (UV) and visible light, respectively. Peak width located in visible light region is ranged from 363 nm to 500 nm, and the central peak is 417 nm that is the central wavelength of violet light. Another two peaks located UV and DUV region is 320 nm and 256 nm, which have narrower peak width and smaller peak intensity than that located in visible light. Hence, although the preparation is very difficult, excellent optical properties of C60 is beneficial to apply to single-quantum-dot light-emitting devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optical properties of GQDs and C60 adsorbing functional groups –OH and -NMP
ce:section.ce:para:
The OH molecules or NMP molecules are attached on top of the C atom in defect-GQDs and defect-C60. The effect of van der Waals interaction between GQDs/C60 and functional group –OH/-NMP can be described by the empirical correction scheme of DFT-D2 (D stands for dispersion) method in Grimme scheme [29]. Defect-GQDs provides three different positions C1, C2 and C3 and defect-C60 provides C1, C2, C3, C4, C5 and C6 six different positions (as shown in Fig.S2). Optimized results show that the distance of defect-GQDs to –OH and -NPM is about 3.0 Å. And the distance of defect-C60 to –OH is about 2.3 Å, as well as the distance of defect-C60 to NMP is about 1.7 Å. It can be seen that the more suitable adsorbing site for defect-GQDs is C1 and for defect-C60 is C5, as shown Fig.S3. The adsorbing energy of the OH molecules or NMP molecules incorporated on defect-GQDs and defect-C60 is calculated by using following formula:
E
a d s
=
E
t o t a l
−
(
E
m o l e c u l e
+
E
s u r f a c e
)
Where
E
a d s
is the adsorption energy;
E
t o t a l
is the energy of the entire system after adsorbing molecules;
E
m o l e c u l e
is the energy of OH/NMP molecule;
E
s u r f a c e
is the energy of defect-GQDs and defect-C60 without adsorption of molecules. Results are shown in Table 2 . It can be seen that the adsorption of OH molecules or NMP molecules on GQDs and C60 with carbon defect surface are very easy. NMP is easier to adsorbed on the surface GQDs and C60 than OH, and defective C60 is more easily decorated by functional groups.

In order to accurately describe electronic structure of functional groups on the surface of GQDs and C60, we adopt the different charge densities [30], as shown in Fig. 4 . Here, oxygen atom in hydroxyl –OH is bond with C, and nitrogen atom in C60 is bond with C. It is clearly seen from Fig. 4(a) that –OH molecules is bond with defect-GQDs and oxygen molecules of –OH molecules will change electrons to GQDs. And hydroxyl –OH is stronger bond with defective C60 as shown in Fig. 4(c). This promotes the elongation and activation of the oxygen molecules of OH. Fig. 4(b) and (d) demonstrates that NMP molecules are bonded with defective -GQDs and –C60. When NMP molecule adsorbed on the defective GQDs surface, the weak interaction precludes charge transfer. The formation of C vacancy defects in C60 is very willing to bond N atom in NMP molecules. And then the adsorption of NMP and OH molecules on defective C60 is stronger than on the defective GQDs surface. NMP have a very strong ability to gain electrons and enhance the interface interaction redistribution. GQDs will receive electrons from adsorbed functional group, which receive 0.11e and 0.17e from –OH and -NMP, respectively. And C60 will give electrons to –OH and -NMP, which are 0.50e and 0.37e, respectively.

Fig. 5 (a) show optical properties for defective GQDs and adsorbing different functional groups defective GQDs. When GQDs form C defect, new adsorbing peak will be formed in lower energy and this peak will be weaken along with functional group adsorbed on the surface of GQDs. For GQDs with C defect, there are still four peaks akin defect-free GQDs, which show clearly blue shift. The investigation of Mombrú et al. [31] have been found that the single-, di- and multivacancy GQDs shift their band gaps to lower energies, which is agreement with our calculation. For GQDs adsorbed functional groups OH, the strength of adsorbing peak (1.94eV) in visible region will enhanced, and several peaks in ultraviolet become gentle. When adsorbing functional group NMP, peak strength in visible region did not change and have clearly blue shift. Other peaks strength in ultraviolet region have clearly intensified and shift forwards more energy. Hence, for large size GQDs, C defect and functional group adsorption can improve its optical properties. By contrast, it can be seen from Fig. 5(b) that C defect and functional group adsorption completely weaken the optical properties of C60, which three strong peaks become flat along with functional group adsorption.

In this work we demonstrate the size effect of quantum dots and discuss effect of defect on the luminescence properties of quantum dots. Calculated formation energy have been shown that the formation of different size GQDs is more stable with the scale increasing. That is, the smaller the GQDs, the more difficult it is to prepared. Results can also been seen that the absorption coefficients of large-size GQDs can be attained in visible range of light and small-size GQDs mainly show the luminescence performance of UV and DUV region. Density of states illustrate the band gap of GQDs become narrower and narrower with increasing size and symmetry. C60 with higher symmetry exhibits better optical and electronic properties. The formation energy of quantum dots adsorbing functional group –OH and -NMP are lower for defective structure than non-defective structure. And the effect of defect and adsorped funtional group on the optical properties of GQDs and C60 is clearly different. For large size GQDs, C defect and functional group adsorption can improve its optical properties. And C defect and functional group adsorption completely weaken its optical properties for C60, which three strong peaks become flat along with functional group adsorption and lose its optical advantage.

Yongqi Chen: Writing-Original Draft, Investigation. Qi Zhang: Writing-Original Draft, WritingReview & Editing. Lingling Bai: Writing Review & Editing. Lingfeng Ye: Data Curation, Zhiping Lin: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing Review & Editing, Data Curation, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Huafeng Dong: Software, Formal analysis. Fugen Wu: Software, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by Guangdong Provincial Natural Science Foundation (No. 2018A030313272) through GDNS Committee of China and was supported by the network computing center of guangdong university of technology.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficient iterative schemes for ab initio total-energy calculations using a plane-wave basis set
